title: Finnish-Inspired Values, Equity and Ethical Positioning
aim: Create a shared professional language for FCEI: Finnish-inspired practice, equity, dignity, access, trust and safe certification wording.
objectives: Explain core Finnish-inspired values using classroom examples, not slogans.
Distinguish ethical CPD positioning from unsupported accreditation claims.
Use evidence before judgement when analysing learner or teacher difficulty.
outcomes: Participants can explain the FCEI values in practical terms.
Participants can identify whether a school action demonstrates or contradicts equity, trust and dignity.
Participants create a values-to-practice pledge for their class or learning centre.

scenarios.label: A
scenarios.problem: A centre advertises “official Finnish qualification” without a confirmed Finnish partner and teachers are unsure how to describe the programme to parents.
flow: 1. Setting / visual scene: show the real classroom or leadership problem without blaming the teacher or learner.
2. Presenter introduces the Finnish-inspired principle linked to equity, access, trust, wellbeing, agency or evidence.
3. Classroom or school problem: name the observable problem and the learner evidence.
4. Teacher/leader response: do not judge; read the evidence first.
5. Step 1 - Support Route: remove the access barrier with visuals, modelling, peer support, movement, language support or environmental adjustment.
6. Step 2 - Core Route: return all learners to the expected standard and shared learning goal.
7. Step 3 - Extension Route: add deeper challenge, transfer, explanation, creativity or leadership for learners ready for more.
8. Formative checkpoint: collect quick evidence through exit ticket, observation note, mini-whiteboard, peer explanation, recording or work sample.
9. Reflection question: ask what the evidence shows and what the teacher will adjust next.
10. Action task: try the strategy in a real lesson and upload a short reflection, learner evidence or revised plan.
Three practical strategies for this scenario:
Barrier-first planning: identify the access barrier before changing the expectation.
Visible success criteria: show what good work looks like through models, rubrics or exemplars.
Evidence pause: stop midway to collect learner evidence and adapt before the lesson ends.
strategies: Barrier-first planning: identify the access barrier before changing the expectation.
Visible success criteria: show what good work looks like through models, rubrics or exemplars.
Evidence pause: stop midway to collect learner evidence and adapt before the lesson ends.

scenarios.label: B
scenarios.problem: A teacher says “I treat everyone the same” but three learners never complete tasks because they cannot access the language of the lesson.
flow: 1. Setting / visual scene: show the real classroom or leadership problem without blaming the teacher or learner.
2. Presenter introduces the Finnish-inspired principle linked to equity, access, trust, wellbeing, agency or evidence.
3. Classroom or school problem: name the observable problem and the learner evidence.
4. Teacher/leader response: do not judge; read the evidence first.
5. Step 1 - Support Route: remove the access barrier with visuals, modelling, peer support, movement, language support or environmental adjustment.
6. Step 2 - Core Route: return all learners to the expected standard and shared learning goal.
7. Step 3 - Extension Route: add deeper challenge, transfer, explanation, creativity or leadership for learners ready for more.
8. Formative checkpoint: collect quick evidence through exit ticket, observation note, mini-whiteboard, peer explanation, recording or work sample.
9. Reflection question: ask what the evidence shows and what the teacher will adjust next.
10. Action task: try the strategy in a real lesson and upload a short reflection, learner evidence or revised plan.
Three practical strategies for this scenario:
Barrier-first planning: identify the access barrier before changing the expectation.
Visible success criteria: show what good work looks like through models, rubrics or exemplars.
Evidence pause: stop midway to collect learner evidence and adapt before the lesson ends.
Participant implementation evidence:
Upload one revised lesson segment, one learner evidence sample, and one 150-word reflection explaining what changed and why.
strategies: Barrier-first planning: identify the access barrier before changing the expectation.
Visible success criteria: show what good work looks like through models, rubrics or exemplars.
Evidence pause: stop midway to collect learner evidence and adapt before the lesson ends.

title: Learner Agency and Joy of Learning
aim: Help teachers create lessons where learners feel active, capable and emotionally connected to learning.
objectives: Design voice and choice moments in ordinary lessons.
Create emotionally positive learning tasks with visible success steps.
Use learner reflection to build agency.
outcomes: Participants redesign one passive task into an active agency task.
Participants write learner-friendly success criteria.
Participants collect one learner reflection sample.

title: Active Learning, Play, Movement and Learning Environment Design
aim: Transform classrooms into spaces where movement, exploration and purposeful interaction support learning.
objectives: Explain active learning, play and movement in education settings.
Plan movement-rich activities connected to learning outcomes.
Arrange the environment to support collaboration, calm routines and access.
outcomes: Participants create a movement-integrated lesson segment.
Participants produce a classroom layout plan for access and collaboration.
Participants identify one barrier in the physical or emotional environment and redesign it.

title: Formative Assessment, Feedback and Self-Assessment
aim: Use assessment to support learning through evidence, feedback, self-assessment and next-step teaching decisions.
objectives: Separate formative evidence from summative judgement.
Create checkpoints that reveal thinking during a lesson.
Write feedback that gives a next action rather than only praise or correction.
outcomes: Participants design three formative checkpoints.
Participants revise one marking comment into a next-step feedback comment.
Participants create a student self-assessment tool.

title: Early Support and the Leave-No-Child-Behind Classroom Cycle
aim: Give educators a practical cycle for noticing learner needs early and providing concrete support without labelling or delay.
objectives: Identify observable learner needs from evidence.
Plan group-specific support before individual withdrawal.
Escalate support ethically when core access support is not enough.
outcomes: Participants complete an early support observation sheet.
Participants design a group support routine.
Participants create a student-specific support plan outline where needed.

title: Mixed-Ability Differentiation: Support/Core/Extension Routes
aim: Show teachers how to keep a common learning goal while varying access, pace, product and challenge.
objectives: Write one core outcome for a mixed-ability lesson.
Design support, core and extension routes.
Use flexible grouping without stigma.
outcomes: Participants redesign one mixed-ability lesson.
Participants prepare a route card set.
Participants collect evidence from three learner levels.

title: Curriculum Adaptation Studio
aim: Train educators to adapt existing coursebooks and units to Finnish-inspired lesson planning without rejecting the curriculum they already use.
objectives: Audit a textbook activity for access, agency, evidence and wellbeing.
Add support/core/extension routes to a non-Finnish unit.
Create a Finnish-inspired lesson sequence in ESL, literature, mathematics and science.
outcomes: Participants complete four adaptation models.
Participants produce one adapted lesson plan from their own context.
Participants explain the rationale behind each adaptation.

title: Phenomenon-Based Projects and Transversal Competence
aim: Build interdisciplinary projects that develop knowledge, skills, collaboration, multiliteracy and sustainable action.
objectives: Define transversal competence in teacher-friendly language.
Design a phenomenon question linked to curriculum objectives.
Include assessment evidence for subject knowledge and broad competences.
outcomes: Participants design a mini phenomenon-based project.
Participants map subject outcomes to transversal competences.
Participants create a student product rubric.

title: Teacher as Researcher and Small Action Research
aim: Make research-minded teaching practical through small cycles of question, evidence, action, review and sharing.
objectives: Turn a classroom concern into an inquiry question.
Collect simple ethical evidence.
Use evidence to improve teaching routines.
outcomes: Participants write a classroom inquiry plan.
Participants collect before-and-after evidence.
Participants share one improvement story with colleagues.

title: Leadership, Quality Culture and Institutional Self-Evaluation
aim: Help owners and leaders build a Finnish-inspired quality culture through trust, evidence, self-evaluation and professional support.
objectives: Use enhancement-led self-evaluation rather than blame-driven inspection.
Create a learning-centre quality dashboard.
Lead professional dialogue using evidence.
outcomes: Participants complete a leadership self-evaluation.
Participants design one centre improvement cycle.
Participants create a staff support plan.

title: SISU Preschool in a Box: Play-Based Early Years Learning
aim: Prepare teachers and parents to use a practical toolkit of games, teaching aids and routines for holistic early years development.
objectives: Explain play-based and active early learning.
Use toolkit materials to support physical, psychological, social, language, mathematical, environmental and creative development.
Design child-led and teacher-guided activities with parent participation.
outcomes: Participants assemble a SISU-style box plan.
Participants run a play-based activity and observe child learning.
Participants create a home-school play card for parents.

title: Blended Learning Implementation and Digital CPD Operations
aim: Turn FCEI into a working blended learning model with online modules, live workshops, implementation tasks and evidence portfolios.
objectives: Design a blended course sequence.
Define evidence uploads, progress rules and tutor feedback routines.
Prepare videos and resources for Colossyan and LMS use.
outcomes: Participants create a blended course delivery plan.
Participants prepare a video-to-task module page.
Participants define certificate evidence requirements.
